common_name: Silver Leaf Philodendron (Brandt's Philodendron, Olive Leaf Philodendron)

planting.note: Grow as a houseplant or shaded patio vine in zone 9; Philodendron brandtianum cannot tolerate frost or temperatures below 60°F. It grows well in a hanging basket as a trailing vine or can be trained up a moss pole to produce progressively larger leaves. Provide a well-draining potting mix with added orchid bark and perlite. Water thoroughly and allow the top inch to dry between waterings. Propagate readily from single-node stem cuttings in water or moist mix. All parts are toxic if chewed.

directions: Grow in bright indirect to moderate shade — Silver Leaf Philodendron is adaptable to lower light levels than many collector philodendrons but produces its best growth and most vibrant silver leaf coloration with consistent bright indirect light. Philodendron brandtianum is a climbing philodendron from Bolivia, Brazil, Colombia, and Peru, valued for its distinctive and unusually colored foliage: the heart-shaped to oval leaves emerge in shades of rich olive-bronze and mature to a striking combination of grayish-green base color overlaid with irregular patches and blotches of silver and pale gray, creating an iridescent, metallic quality in the leaf that is unusual among philodendrons. The contrast between the olive-toned base and the silvery markings is most visible in raking or indirect light and varies somewhat depending on growing conditions. The leaves are smaller than many philodendrons (typically 4–8 inches), giving the plant a neater, more compact form well-suited to hanging baskets and medium-sized containers. New leaves emerge on vivid red-pink petioles that fade to green as the leaf matures, adding further ornamental interest. Philodendron brandtianum is generally regarded as more adaptable and easier to grow than most collector aroids, tolerating imperfect humidity and temperature fluctuation with less damage to its appearance than the demanding velvet types. All parts toxic due to calcium oxalate.
sources: Royal Botanic Gardens Kew — Philodendron brandtianum species notes
University of Florida IFAS Extension — Philodendron species for indoor tropical culture
American Horticulture Society — Philodendron brandtianum ornamental characteristics
ASPCA Animal Poison Control — Philodendron (toxic to pets)

common_name: Zebra Alocasia (Zebra Elephant Ear, Tiger Leg Alocasia)

planting.note: Grow as a houseplant or shaded container in zone 9; Alocasia zebrina cannot tolerate frost or temperatures below 60°F. Use a well-draining mix with added perlite; Alocasias are prone to root rot in heavy soils. Allow the top inch to dry between waterings. The plant may go dormant (losing leaves) during cool or low-light winter months; resume watering when new growth appears. Propagate by separating offsets (pups) from the base of the main plant. All parts are toxic.

directions: Grow in bright indirect to moderate shade — Zebra Alocasia maintains its most vibrant appearance with consistent bright indirect light and will become etiolated and lose leaf intensity in very dim conditions. Alocasia zebrina is a striking tropical aroid native to the Philippines in the family Araceae, but its most distinctive ornamental feature is not the leaves alone but the combination of leaf and stem: the plant's petioles (leaf stalks) are dramatically marked with alternating bands and stripes of pale cream-yellow and dark olive-green in a bold pattern that precisely resembles the markings of a zebra — giving the plant its common name 'Zebra Alocasia,' 'Tiger Leg,' or 'Zebra Stem Elephant Ear.' The large, arrowhead-shaped leaves (typically 12–18 inches on established plants) are glossy, medium-green with a smooth surface, elevated on the uniquely patterned long petioles that form the primary ornamental display. The combination of large glossy arrowhead leaves and eye-catching banded stems creates a tropical accent plant with strong architectural presence. Alocasia zebrina is more tolerant of indoor conditions than many Alocasia species and is a good choice for collectors new to the genus. Like all Alocasias, it produces corms (small underground bulblets) that can be separated and grown into new plants. All parts toxic due to calcium oxalate crystals.
sources: University of Florida IFAS Extension — Alocasia species for tropical indoor culture
Royal Horticultural Society — Alocasia zebrina cultivation notes
American Horticulture Society — Alocasia zebrina: zebra stem characteristics
ASPCA Animal Poison Control — Alocasia (toxic to pets)

common_name: Squirrel's Foot Fern (Ball Fern, Rabbit's Paw Fern)

planting.note: Grow in a hanging basket or on a moss ball/kokedama in bright indirect to moderate shade; the creeping rhizomes of Squirrel's Foot Fern naturally cover the outside of containers and hanging baskets, creating a striking shaggy-rhizome display. Do not bury the rhizomes under growing medium — they need to be exposed on the surface. Water thoroughly and allow to approach dryness between waterings (more drought-tolerant than most ferns due to its epiphytic roots). Propagate by cutting rhizome sections with fronds attached and pressing them to moist growing medium.

directions: Grow in bright indirect to moderate shade — Squirrel's Foot Fern is one of the most shade-tolerant and drought-forgiving of the epiphytic ferns, making it an ideal choice for indoor hanging baskets in low to moderate light. Davallia trichomanoides is a small, epiphytic fern native to Malaysia, Java, and Borneo in Asia, growing naturally on tree bark and rocky outcrops in humid tropical forests. The plant's defining and immediately captivating feature is its rhizomes: thick, creeping, furry brown surface rhizomes that radiate outward from the center of the plant and cascade down and over the edges of containers, covered in dense, soft, pale brown to rust-colored scales that give them the precise texture and appearance of a small squirrel's or rabbit's foot — the quality that inspired the plant's common names. From these distinctive rhizomes emerge small, finely divided, triangular, lacy fronds of deep medium-green that create an attractive, textured canopy above the showier rhizomes. The combination of the ornamental creeping rhizomes and the finely cut fern fronds makes Davallia trichomanoides particularly striking in hanging baskets, where both elements can be fully appreciated from below. Unlike most ferns, it tolerates periodic drying of the fronds and can be revived from apparent dessication by a thorough soaking. Non-toxic to pets.
sources: Royal Horticultural Society — Davallia trichomanoides cultivation
University of Florida IFAS Extension — Epiphytic ferns for indoor culture
American Fern Society — Davallia species: rabbit's foot ferns
ASPCA Animal Poison Control — Davallia (non-toxic to pets)

planting.note: Grow as a houseplant or greenhouse specimen in bright indirect light with very high humidity (70–80%) and exceptional drainage; Anthurium warocqueanum is strictly a tropical collector's plant in zone 9 and requires the closest approximation of Colombian cloud forest conditions — consistently warm temperatures (65–80°F), very high humidity, excellent air circulation, and bright filtered light. Mount on a bark slab or grow in a hanging basket to allow the pendant leaves to hang freely. All parts are toxic if ingested.

directions: Grow in bright indirect to moderate shade in very high humidity — Queen Anthurium requires the consistently warm, humid conditions of its native Colombian cloud forest habitat and will not tolerate the dry air of typical home environments without supplemental humidification. Anthurium warocqueanum is a large epiphytic anthurium from the humid cloud forests of Colombia, named in honor of the Belgian botanist and collector M. Warocqué, and commonly called the 'Queen Anthurium' — reigning alongside Anthurium veitchii (the 'King') as one of the two most desirable large-leaved pendulous anthuriums in collector circles. The leaves are extraordinary: enormously elongated, strap-shaped, pendant, dark emerald-green, velvety, with a precisely delineated network of pale silvery-white veins, reaching 3–5 feet on mature specimens. The velvety texture results from microscopic papillae that scatter light, creating the characteristic soft, deep, matte surface. Unlike Anthurium veitchii, whose leaves are smooth and corrugated, the Queen Anthurium's leaves are velvety and soft to the touch, and the silver venation is more pronounced and ornate. Both species require a mounted or elevated growing position to allow the pendant leaves to hang freely. Anthurium warocqueanum is considered among the most difficult of the collector aroids to grow to its full potential, requiring extremely consistent conditions, but mature specimens with fully-developed pendant velvety leaves are considered among the finest of all tropical foliage plants. All parts are toxic.
sources: Royal Botanic Gardens Kew — Anthurium warocqueanum species profile
Missouri Botanical Garden — Anthurium warocqueanum: Queen Anthurium
University of Florida IFAS Extension — Collector Anthurium species for indoor culture
ASPCA Animal Poison Control — Anthurium (toxic to pets)

common_name: Clog Plant (Guppy Plant, Goldfish Plant, Pouch Flower)

planting.note: Grow as a hanging basket or trailing container in bright indirect light to partial shade; Clog Plant cannot tolerate frost or temperatures below 55°F. It blooms most freely with a brief, dry, slightly cool rest period (6–8 weeks at 55–60°F with reduced watering) in fall before resuming regular care in winter-spring for peak bloom. Propagate from stem-tip cuttings in moist mix. Use a fast-draining, slightly acidic potting mix. Water thoroughly and allow to approach dryness between waterings — the thick stems store water and are susceptible to rot in heavy media.

directions: Grow in bright indirect to moderate shade as a hanging basket or trailing container plant — Clog Plant flowers most prolifically in bright indirect light (a bright east or north window) and will produce few flowers in dim conditions. Nematanthus gregarius (formerly Hypocyrta radicans and various other synonyms) is a trailing, semi-succulent epiphytic vine from the coastal Atlantic Forest of Brazil in the Gesneriaceae family, closely related to Columnea and Aeschynanthus. The common names — 'Clog Plant,' 'Pouch Flower,' 'Guppy Plant' — all refer to the distinctive shape of the flowers: small (half an inch to an inch), bright orange to orange-red, balloon-shaped or pouch-shaped with a small, pinched opening at one end, the overall silhouette remarkably resembling a tiny Dutch wooden clog, a bloated tropical fish, or a miniature water balloon. The flowers emerge from the leaf axils along the trailing stems in succession throughout the warm months, often blanketing an established hanging basket in dozens of the tiny orange pouches simultaneously. The stems are clothed in small, fleshy, shiny, dark-green, opposite leaves that have a bright, glossy quality. Clog Plant is one of the most rewarding of the trailing gesneriads for indoor hanging baskets — reliably blooming for months once established in appropriate conditions. Non-toxic to pets.
sources: American Gesneriad Society — Nematanthus gregarius (Clog Plant) care guide
Royal Horticultural Society — Nematanthus cultivation notes
University of Florida IFAS Extension — Gesneriad houseplants for Florida
ASPCA Animal Poison Control — Nematanthus (non-toxic to pets)
